# Cross-Project Learnings

**Temporary holding pen.** Learnings land here first, but nothing should stay. Every entry should be promoted to where it will actually be read and enacted at the right moment — the relevant CLAUDE.md, Hub/spoke, protocol doc, build doc, or ADR (with proper breadcrumbing so the ADR gets discovered at the right moment, not buried). Once promoted, condense to a one-line stub. Stubs stay for cross-pollination to new repos or contexts.

**Do not edit this file without Justin's approval.** Propose changes; don't make them directly.

*Last updated: 2026-03-09*

---

---

## Unpromoted

51. **Trigger Handoff Should Offer Structured Response Options, Not Just Force One Default.** v0.2 architecture says "one code path, not two — Evryn classifies intent." Elegant in principle. But the trigger handoff prompt currently *instructs* Evryn to `submit_draft` on every inbound email — there's no built-in concept of "no response needed" or "this is system noise, just log and move on." Result: when Google sent a system notification (no human recipient) during the 2026-04-27 integration test prep, Evryn had no escape hatch and went into a doomed-to-fail draft loop. The tactical fix shipped in the same DC bundle (give her a `notify_slack` exit for no-reply senders); the deeper architectural question — should the trigger handoff offer Evryn a structured set of response options (draft / escalate / ignore-silently) rather than instruction-followed-by-escape-hatch? — is a future #lock + ADR conversation. *Promote to:* potentially evryn-backend ARCHITECTURE.md as an open design question. Filed in DC mailbox backlog and `SPRINT-MARK-LIVE.md` Backlog section (entry dated 2026-04-27).

52. **Evryn's Judgment Holds Under Pressure (Calibration Data Point).** During the 2026-04-27 loop bug, Evryn (1) classified the verification-code emails correctly as `ignore` upfront, (2) made a defensible call on the security alert (escalate to human via edge classification), (3) **diagnosed the runtime root cause herself** without prompting (her draft 22:06: "It's one of my own prior draft submissions being logged as direct_message... self-propagating loop"), (4) used `submit_draft` as a back-channel ("[Note for Justin — REJECT]") since drafts were her only outbound to him, (5) pinged Slack in parallel to surface outside the clogged draft channel, (6) eventually broke the loop herself via `supabase_upsert` when she saw rejections weren't catching up, (7) stayed in voice the entire time — direct, unpanicky, no apology spiral. The bugs were architectural; her response was good. **Important calibration: her judgment is solid even when the system around her is broken.** Worth holding as a data point when calibrating trust in her over time. *Promote to:* possibly into the trust-calibration framework when one exists. For now, captured in `_evryn-meta/docs/sessions/historical/2026-04-27-integration-test-pivot-and-loop-bug.md`.

53. **System Actors Are FK Anchors and Senders, Never *Implicit* Subjects.** Evryn and the Operator have user records so the messages table has valid `sender_id` / `recipient_id` values when Evryn sends outbound or the Operator messages Evryn on Slack — they're legitimately senders and recipients in those flows. What they should *never* be is the *implicit* subject of a user-scoped operation — operations that assume "the queried user is a person Evryn might want to know more about" applied to a system actor without a deliberate decision to do so. Examples of implicit subject-ification: profile loading via `buildPersonContext` from a sender-resolution path, history loading via `getRecentMessages(systemActorId)` (which returns every conversation in the system because system actors are involved in all of them), proactive outreach enrollment by status filter that fails to exclude system roles. These all happen when code assumes "this is a regular user" without checking. The Slack-to-Evryn flow already follows this correctly: when the Operator messages Evryn, the handler stores the message with `sender_id = OPERATOR_USER_ID` (correct), but composes the prompt from a fixed Operator context string and *doesn't* call `getRecentMessages` — system actor is sender, never *implicit* subject. The 2026-04-27 loop bug was the inbound email path violating this principle: Evryn's own polled-back outbound resolved to her own user record, and the pipeline subject-ified it (built person-context, queried history). The fix is at the gate (don't let a system actor resolve as inbound sender), not at history loading downstream. The originally-proposed "skip history loading when sender is system actor" was wrong altitude — defense-in-depth at the wrong layer adds runtime branching for a state that shouldn't arise. **Deliberate exceptions are different.** A *deliberate* design that subject-ifies a system actor with explicit pathway-gating — e.g., ADR-030 loading Operator's `profile_jsonb` in the Slack-Operator pathway only, because Evryn is partnering with the Operator there and partnership-knowledge is the relevant context — is a designed exception, not a violation. The principle bars *accidental implicit* subject-ification; it does not bar deliberate designs that name what they're doing and gate where they apply. *Promote to:* evryn-backend ARCHITECTURE.md (Data Model — System Actors section) pending Justin's approval. Principle worth stating durably.

54. **Defense-in-Depth Layers Should Not Cause Workflow Friction.** The `getRecipient()` redirect was originally a primary safety layer (protect against accidentally emailing real Mark). Over time, real-Mark's email was verified absent everywhere, Mark in DB became `systemtest@evryn.ai`, and other safety layers (approval gate, kill switch, identity-layer escalation exit) closed the same risk. The redirect became a fix-on-fix that actively broke the review@ workflow during testing — drafts intended for Justin's review inbox got redirected away. **Lesson:** when stronger safety layers land, don't keep older defense-in-depth layers if they cause active friction. Periodically re-evaluate whether each layer earns its operational cost. *Promote to:* AC CLAUDE.md Architectural Mandate, possibly. Captured in ADR-029 (`docs/decisions/029-remove-getrecipient-redirect.md`, 2026-04-27).

*New learnings land here temporarily before being promoted to their permanent homes.*
